sequence.headline: Create an rbcL sequence for the generalized Rubisco expression platform
sequence.subtitle: Enter a common or scientific plant name. The app resolves it through UniProt taxonomy, finds the corresponding rbcL protein entry, and returns the amino acid sequence in FASTA format.
sequence.footer: Uses UniProt taxonomy search plus the UniProtKB REST API. If a plant name is ambiguous, the app picks the best plant-like taxonomy match and then searches for gene:rbcL within that taxon.

dryLab.headline: See how different proteins score in their ability to be assembled by a generalized expression platform<
dryLab.subtitle: For each plant in this directory, we have calculated a score based on seven key dry lab metrics. These metrics include factors such as stability, mutational burden, and, most important, similarity to the experimentally successful Rubisco mutants tested by Cai et al. (spinach, Flaveria, and tobacco). This score represents the likelihood that a given plant’s Rubisco protein will be assembled correctly in E. coli. The hope is that these scores can guide the selection of plants for in vitro tests and will be updated as further wet lab testing occurs.
dryLab.methodology: Select a plant from the dropdown to view the overall compatibility score and further information on the seven metrics.

stability.title: Stability Score (ΔΔG)
stability.what: ΔΔG describes the predicted change in protein stability caused by the N-terminal substitution. It compares the mutant RbcL with the original plant protein. A negative score means that the mutant is less stable than the wild type.
stability.why: If the N-terminal mutation makes RbcL much less stable, the protein may be harder to fold correctly or keep stable once it is produced in E. coli.
stability.how: ΔΔG was calculated using the DynaMut2 web portal. The wild-type rbcL was loaded and the substition was represented as 76 simultaneous mutations using the multiple mutations option.
stability.hero_label: ΔΔG changes between the wild-type (red) and mutant (yellow) rbcLs

core_fold.title: Core Structural Preservation
core_fold.what: This metric measures the root mean square deviation between the wild-type and mutant RbcL structures. A low RMSD indicates that the proteins are structurally similar.
core_fold.why: If the mutant structure is significantly different from the wild type, it may behave differently, particularly in its catalytic behaviour. Keeping the catalytic core as unchanged as possible is especially important for Rubisco, as its catalytic parameters are most frequently studied.
core_fold.how: The mutant and wild-type models were aligned in PyMol. The output included both unpruned RMSD, which reflects the whole protein, and pruned RMSD, which focuses on the conserved core.

interface.title: Holoenzyme Interface Preservation
interface.what: The combination of rbcL and rbcS produces the L8S8 holoenzyme structure, a formation which relies on strong protein-protein interfaces within the structure. This metric quantifies the maximum change in interface surface areas wihin the holoenzyme due to the mutant rbcL.
interface.why: The purpose of this mutant is to enable easier assembly, so it is critical that the N-terminal change does not badly disrupt the interfaces needed to form the L8S8 holoenzyme. If there is a significant drop in interface surface area (>15% loss), the interface may be too weak to form.
interface.how: Holoenzyme models were built in PyMOL by placing the mutant RbcL into the original holoenzyme and removing the original RbcL. PISA was then used to compare interface areas across the protein-protein contacts.

nterm_similarity.title: N-terminal Structural Similarity to a Successful Construct
nterm_similarity.what: This metric compares the N-terminal structure of the mutant construct to a successful N-terminal mutant reported in the literature.
nterm_similarity.why: Even with the same amino acid sequence from Synechococcus, the N-terminus can fold slightly differently in different plant backbones. If the shape of the N-terminus is similar to a known successful construct (i.e. from spinach, Flaveria, or tobacco), the general chaperones are more likely to recognize and bind to it in the same way.
nterm_similarity.how: The first 76 N-terminal residues from the mutant and the successful spinach, Flaveria, and tobacco constructs were isolated and aligned in PyMOL. The RMSD from the alignment was then recorded for each and the lowest RMSD was chosen.

nterm_surface.title: N-terminal Surface Area Similarity to a Successful Construct
nterm_surface.what: This metric measures the percentage change in surface area between the mutant N-terminus and that of a successful construct reported in the literature.
nterm_surface.why: Surface area gives an another way to compare the part of the protein that the chaperones will bind to during assembly. A drastic reduction in surface area as compared to successful constructs would mean that the chaperones have less N-terminal area to bind to, likely resulting in less successful assembly.
nterm_surface.how: The N-terminal regions were isolated in PyMOL and their surface areas were measured and compared. Percentage difference in surface area was computed between the mutant and the successful N-termini and the highest positive value was chosen.
nterm_surface.hero_label: Wild-type (green) and mutant (yellow) N-termini surfaces superimposed

mutation_burden.title: Conservation-Weighted Mutation Burden
mutation_burden.what: This metric estimates how disruptive the N-terminal substitution is across the sequence overall. It combines the conservation of each residue with the disruptiveness of the mutation made.
mutation_burden.why: Significant mutations to a sequence can be damaging to protein, especially if that mutation replaces a highly conserved residue across plant evolutionary history or makes a particularly disruptive residue change. This score quantifies the burden brought on by the 76-residue mutation, which is different between plants.
mutation_burden.how: A multiple sequence alignment created using ConSurf is used to measure conservation at each residue position, and the BLOSUM62 matrix is used to score the substitution itself. The two values are multipled and the score for each residue is summed across the 76-residue replacement. A lower score indicates a lower mutational burden.

codon_adaptation.title: Codon Adaptation Score
codon_adaptation.what: This metric measures the codon adaptation index, or CAI, for the mutant RbcL gene. It quantifies how closely the sequence matches the codon usage that E. coli prefers.
codon_adaptation.why: Even a well-designed protein must be expressed efficiently. If the producing cell (i.e. E. coli) can easily read the gene and frequently uses the associated codon, it will produce more of the protein for testing.
codon_adaptation.how: The modified RbcL sequence was reverse translated and codon-optimized for E. coli before being passed through a CAI calculator, which determines the rarity of the codons involved. A lower score indicates the use of more rare codons, making it more difficult for E. coli to express the protein.

arabidopsis.label: Arabidopsis thaliana
arabidopsis.overall_summary: Arabidopsis thaliana is a well-established model plant. However, the dry lab predicts only a moderate probability of successful Rubisco assembly and activity in the generalized platform.

stability.interpretation: A ΔΔG of -2.49 is within the range that was successful in experimental testing, indicating that this is a sufficiently small decrease in stability.

core_fold.interpretation: The pruned RMSD was quite high at 0.756 Å, showing that the main core structure deviated somewhat from the wild type. This is part of the reason for the lower score, as the catalytic machinery of rbcL may not be as well-preserved following the N-terminal substitution and therefore less representative of the actual catalytic properties of Arabidopsis Rubisco.

interface.interpretation: The largest decrease in interface area was 25.46%, which is a fairly significant decrease. This indicates it may have difficulty assembling, contributing to its lower score.

nterm_similarity.interpretation: The N-terminus of the mutant Arabidopsis rbcL most closely aligned with that of the successful Flaveria rbcL. This close alignment indicates that the Arabidopsis N-terminus is also likely to successfully bind to the general chaperone set.

nterm_surface.interpretation: The Arabidopsis N-terminal surface area is essentially identical to that of Flaveria, again indicating that it is likely to bind well to the chaperones.
nterm_surface.technical_details: PyMOL surface-area comparison of the isolated N-terminal region.

mutation_burden.interpretation: A mutation burden of 546 is within the acceptable range, as other plant Rubisco proteins were successfully assembled in vitro with similar mutational burdens.

codon_adaptation.interpretation: This sequence is well-optimized for E. coli with a CAI of 0.89. This is similar to that of other Rubisco proteins which were successfully produced in E. coli.

flaveria.label: Flaveria bidentis
flaveria.overall_summary: The modified Flaveria has already been shown to assemble successfully with the generalized platform. Its scores are here for comparison.

stability.interpretation: Since Flaveria assembled successfully in vitro, a drop in stability as low as -2.49 is acceptable.

core_fold.interpretation: The pruned RMSD was only 0.389 Å, showing that the main core structure stayed very close to the wild type. This is important because the catalytic machinery sits in this conserved region.
core_fold.technical_details: ChimeraX alignment with both pruned and unpruned RMSD values reported.

interface.interpretation: A holoenzyme model for Flaveria bidentis has not been published, so this metric cannot be computed
interface.technical_details: PyMOL model reconstruction followed by PISA interface comparison.

nterm_similarity.interpretation: The metric involves comparing to the successful Flaveria N-terminus, but since this is Flaveria, this is not meaningful. A base score of 1 was given.
nterm_similarity.technical_details: PyMOL alignment of residues 1-76 against the successful Flaveria construct.

nterm_surface.interpretation: As before, a comparison cannot be made between Flaveria and itself. A base score of 1 was given.
nterm_surface.technical_details: PyMOL surface-area comparison of the isolated N-terminal region.

mutation_burden.interpretation: This metric is primarily useful when comparing across species. More information will be added as more plants are added.
mutation_burden.technical_details: Conservation weighting from an RbcL MSA plus BLOSUM62 substitution penalties.

codon_adaptation.interpretation: This sequence was surprisingly well-optimized for E. coli with a CAI of 0.9. This may have been part of the reason it was selected for experimental study.
codon_adaptation.technical_details: Reverse translation, codon optimization for E. coli, and CAI calculation.

tobacco.label: Nicotania tabacum (tobacco)
tobacco.overall_summary: The modified tobacco has already been shown to assemble successfully with the generalized platform. Its scores are here for comparison.

stability.technical_details: FoldX stability comparison on predicted wild-type and mutant structures.

core_fold.interpretation: The pruned RMSD was only 0.475 Å, showing that the main core structure stayed very close to the wild type. This is important because the catalytic machinery sits in this conserved region.
core_fold.technical_details: ChimeraX alignment with both pruned and unpruned RMSD values reported.

interface.interpretation: The largest decrease in interface area was 5.17% between substructures A and E. This is a fairly small change, which indicates why it was successful in when tested in the lab.
interface.technical_details: PyMOL model reconstruction followed by PISA interface comparison.

nterm_similarity.interpretation: The metric involves comparing to the successful tobacco N-terminus, but since this is tobacco, this is not meaningful. A base score of 1 was given.
nterm_similarity.technical_details: PyMOL alignment of residues 1-76 against the successful tobacco construct.

nterm_surface.interpretation: As before, a comparison cannot be made between tobacco and itself. A base score of 1 was given.
nterm_surface.technical_details: PyMOL surface-area comparison of the isolated N-terminal region.

mutation_burden.interpretation: This metric is primarily useful when comparing across species. More information will be added as more plants are added.
mutation_burden.technical_details: Conservation weighting from an RbcL MSA plus BLOSUM62 substitution penalties.

codon_adaptation.interpretation: This sequence was surprisingly well-optimized for E. coli with a CAI of 1.00. This may have been part of the reason it was selected for experimental study.
codon_adaptation.technical_details: Reverse translation, codon optimization for E. coli, and CAI calculation.

spinach.label: Spinacia oleracea (spinach)
spinach.overall_summary: The modified spinach has already been shown to assemble successfully with the generalized platform. Its scores are here for comparison.

stability.technical_details: FoldX stability comparison on predicted wild-type and mutant structures.

core_fold.interpretation: The pruned RMSD was only 0.413 Å, showing that the main core structure stayed very close to the wild type. That is important because the catalytic machinery sits in this conserved region.
core_fold.technical_details: ChimeraX alignment with both pruned and unpruned RMSD values reported.

interface.interpretation: The largest decrease in interface area was 15.76% between substructures A and E. This is a comparatively large change, but since this was successful experimentally, it demonstrates that interface area changes as high as 15% are acceptable.
interface.technical_details: PyMOL model reconstruction followed by PISA interface comparison.

nterm_similarity.interpretation: The metric involves comparing to the successful spinach N-terminus, but since this is spinach, this is not meaningful. A base score of 1 was given.
nterm_similarity.technical_details: PyMOL alignment of residues 1-76 against the successful spinach construct.

nterm_surface.interpretation: As before, a comparison cannot be made between spinach and itself. A base score of 1 was given.
nterm_surface.technical_details: PyMOL surface-area comparison of the isolated N-terminal region.

mutation_burden.interpretation: This metric is primarily useful when comparing across species. More information will be added as more plants are added.
mutation_burden.technical_details: Conservation weighting from an RbcL MSA plus BLOSUM62 substitution penalties.

codon_adaptation.interpretation: This sequence was surprisingly well-optimized for E. coli with a CAI of 1.00. This may have been part of the reason it was selected for experimental study.
codon_adaptation.technical_details: Reverse translation, codon optimization for E. coli, and CAI calculation.

rice.label: Oryza sativa (rice)
rice.overall_summary: The modified rice Rubisco is predicted to be highly compatible with the generalized expression platform.

stability.interpretation: The mutant rice RbcL has a positive ΔΔG, which suggests that the engineered protein is not obviously destabilized and may even be slightly more stable than the wild type. That is a good sign for folding in E. coli.
stability.technical_details: FoldX stability comparison on predicted wild-type and mutant structures.

core_fold.interpretation: The unpruned RMSD was 3.706 Å, which is high but expected because the N-terminus has been changed. The pruned RMSD was only 0.436 Å, showing that the main core structure stayed very close to the wild type. That is important because the catalytic machinery sits in this conserved region.
core_fold.technical_details: ChimeraX alignment with both pruned and unpruned RMSD values reported.

interface.interpretation: The largest decrease in interface area was 5.83% between substructures A and D. That is a small change, so the mutation is unlikely to seriously interfere with holoenzyme assembly.
interface.technical_details: PyMOL model reconstruction followed by PISA interface comparison.

nterm_similarity.interpretation: The rice and spinach N-termini align very closely, with an RMSD of 0.347 Å. That suggests the rice construct presents a similar recognition surface to the successful spinach example, which is a promising sign for assembly.
nterm_similarity.technical_details: PyMOL alignment of residues 1-76 against the successful spinach construct.

nterm_surface.interpretation: The rice mutant differs from the successful spinach mutant by less than 0.5% in surface area. That means the two N-termini expose almost the same amount of surface, so the chaperones should see a very similar target.
nterm_surface.technical_details: PyMOL surface-area comparison of the isolated N-terminal region.

mutation_burden.interpretation: This metric is primarily useful when comparing across species. More information will be added as more plants are added.
mutation_burden.technical_details: Conservation weighting from an RbcL MSA plus BLOSUM62 substitution penalties.

codon_adaptation.interpretation: A CAI of 0.708 is fairly high, which suggests that E. coli should be able to produce the engineered RbcL sequence reasonably well.
codon_adaptation.technical_details: Reverse translation, codon optimization for E. coli, and CAI calculation.
